subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 06, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - The use of oXiris hemofilter in the initial continuous renal r
sentences: Story 1 is about The use of oXiris hemofilter in the initial continuous renal replacement therapy among patients with septic shock: A propensity score matched analysis..
This is a recent research article from Journal of critical care.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adult patients with septic shock, who were admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) of our tertiary referral hospital between 2019 and 2023, and underwent at least one CRRT session, how should we interpret ients who met all eligibility criteria, 69 cases in the oXiris group were matched with 69 individuals in the M100 group compared with the M100 group, the use of oXiris CRRT was associated with longer 28-day vasopressor-free days (24 vs. 19 days, P = 0.04?
Of 465 patients who met all eligibility criteria, 69 cases in the oXiris group were matched with 69 individuals in the M100 group.
19 days, P = 0.04), shorter CRRT duration (72 vs.
Furthermore, no significant between-group differences were observed in changes in lactate, PCT, or IL-6 levels, fluid balance, ICU or hospital length of stay (LOS), or short-term mortality.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Meta-analysis of the prognostic value of skin perfusion parame
sentences: Story 2 is about Meta-analysis of the prognostic value of skin perfusion parameters in sepsis patients: Evidence integration based on the Mottling Score, capillary refill time, and peripheral perfusion index..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Shock (Augusta, Ga.).

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients (primary outcomes: 28-day mortality, 14-day mortality, and in-hospital mortality) were included, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

The meta-analysis results demonstrated that: (1) Mottling Score (MS), was significantly associated with 28-day mortality (Odds Ratio OR=2.27, 95% Confidence Interval CI: 1.79, 2.87, P<0.001) and 14-day mortality (OR=1.96, 95% CI: 1.32, 2.91, P<0.001); (2) Prolonged Capillary Refill Time (CRT) duration was significantly associated with 28-day mortality (OR=3.29, 95% CI: 2.08, 5.21, P<0.001) and in-hospital mortality (OR=2.46, 95% CI: 1.18, 5.12, P<0.001); (3) Reduced Peripheral Perfusion Index (PPI) was significantly associated with 28-day mortality (OR=5.05, 95% CI: 3.65, 6.98, P<0.001) and in-hospital mortality (OR=4.56, 95% CI: 3.32, 6.26, P<0.001).
This study included a total of 22 articles, encompassing 2,727 study subjects.
Sensitivity analysis confirmed robust results with no significant publication bias.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.

The bedside implication is this: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - High-dose corticosteroids are associated with higher mortality
sentences: Story 3 is about High-dose corticosteroids are associated with higher mortality in patients with COVID-19 ARDS: Results from a nationwide observational study..
This is a recent research article from Journal of critical care.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adult patients with COVID-19 ARDS, defined according to Berlin criteria for ARDS, across 22 centers in the Netherlands between March 2020 and January 2021, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Data from 848 patients were analyzed: 378(44.6%) received high-dose and 470 (55.4%) standard-dose corticosteroids.
Among those treated with high-dose corticosteroids, 63 (16.7%) started therapy within the first day after ICU admission, and 315(83.3%) started at a median of 9 days(IQR = 4-14) after admission.

During a median 28 days of follow-up, 183 patients in the high-dose and 154 in the standard-dose group died [incident rate = 2.12 per 100 person-days, 95% confidence interval (CI) = 1.81-2.43 and 1.41 per 100 person-days, 95%CI = 1.13-1.63, respectively].
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: The use of oXiris hemofilter in the initial continuous renal replacement therapy among patients with septic shock: A propensity score matched analysis.
articles.summary: Of 465 patients who met all eligibility criteria, 69 cases in the oXiris group were matched with 69 individuals in the M100 group. 19 days, P = 0.04), shorter CRRT duration (72 vs. Furthermore, no significant between-group differences were observed in changes in lactate, PCT, or IL-6 levels, fluid balance, ICU or hospital length of stay (LOS), or short-term mortality. The impact of continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) with oXiris hemofilter (oXiris CRRT) on sepsis outcomes remain controversial. We aimed to investigate the association between the oXiris CRRT and subsequent outcomes in adult patients with septic shock.

Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.

articles.title: Meta-analysis of the prognostic value of skin perfusion parameters in sepsis patients: Evidence integration based on the Mottling Score, capillary refill time, and peripheral perfusion index.
articles.summary: Cohort studies or case-control studies investigating the correlation between skin perfusion parameters and the prognosis of sepsis patients (primary outcomes: 28-day mortality, 14-day mortality, and in-hospital mortality) were included. The meta-analysis results demonstrated that: (1) Mottling Score (MS), was significantly associated with 28-day mortality (Odds Ratio OR=2.27, 95% Confidence Interval CI: 1.79, 2.87, P<0.001) and 14-day mortality (OR=1.96, 95% CI: 1.32, 2.91, P<0.001); (2) Prolonged Capillary Refill Time (CRT) duration was significantly associated with 28-day mortality (OR=3.29, 95% CI: 2.08, 5.21, P<0.001) and in-hospital mortality (OR=2.46, 95% CI: 1.18, 5.12, P<0.001); (3) Reduced Peripheral Perfusion Index (PPI) was significantly associated with 28-day mortality (OR=5.05, 95% CI: 3.65, 6.98, P<0.001) and in-hospital mortality (OR=4.56, 95% CI: 3.32, 6.26, P<0.001). Despite advances in intensive care, sepsis and septic shock still have high morbidity and mortality. Microcirculatory disturbance is a key issue in sepsis, leading to hypoperfusion, hypoxia, and organ failure. The skin is a sensitive indicator of microcirculatory changes and often shows early systemic changes.
articles.analysis: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.

This study included a total of 22 articles, encompassing 2,727 study subjects.
Sensitivity analysis confirmed robust results with no significant publication bias.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.

articles.title: High-dose corticosteroids are associated with higher mortality in patients with COVID-19 ARDS: Results from a nationwide observational study.
articles.summary: Mortality hazards were compared between patients receiving high-dose (>6 mg dexamethasone or equivalent) and standard-dose (6 mg dexamethasone or equivalent) corticosteroids using marginal structural models to adjust for time-varying confounding related to initiation of high-dose therapy. Data from 848 patients were analyzed: 378(44.6%) received high-dose and 470 (55.4%) standard-dose corticosteroids. Among those treated with high-dose corticosteroids, 63 (16.7%) started therapy within the first day after ICU admission, and 315(83.3%) started at a median of 9 days(IQR = 4-14) after admission. During a median 28 days of follow-up, 183 patients in the high-dose and 154 in the standard-dose group died [incident rate = 2.12 per 100 person-days, 95% confidence interval (CI) = 1.81-2.43 and 1.41 per 100 person-days, 95%CI = 1.13-1.63, respectively]. In the marginal structural model, high-dose corticosteroids were associated with increased mortality (hazard ratio = 2.45, 95%CI = 1.97-3.05).
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

keyResults: Data from 848 patients were analyzed: 378(44.6%) received high-dose and 470 (55.4%) standard-dose corticosteroids.
Among those treated with high-dose corticosteroids, 63 (16.7%) started therapy within the first day after ICU admission, and 315(83.3%) started at a median of 9 days(IQR = 4-14) after admission.

During a median 28 days of follow-up, 183 patients in the high-dose and 154 in the standard-dose group died [incident rate = 2.12 per 100 person-days, 95% confidence interval (CI) = 1.81-2.43 and 1.41 per 100 person-days, 95%CI = 1.13-1.63, respectively].
In the marginal structural model, high-dose corticosteroids were associated with increased mortality (hazard ratio = 2.45, 95%CI = 1.97-3.05).
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
